text: The laparoscopic view shows the starting point for bladder dissection in a scarred bladder due to multiple previous caesarean sections. The corneal structures have been dissected away, revealing the bladder adherent to the lower uterine segment as a band-like scar area. The uterine artery and vein are visible running laterally to the cervix. The surgeon identifies the correct plane of dissection below the scarred area where the bladder is not adherent.

The most common mistake is to try to approach the bladder right from the center of the scar area or from the side at the level of the scar area itself but here the surgeon will have to cut through an already formed area of fibrosis which will result in bleeding and ultimately in the surgeon getting exhausted trying to find the correct plane of dissection and giving up or causing a complication on the way. This correct plane of dissection was first described by Professor Shirish Sheth, past president FIGO, in which he postulated that the central part of the bladder may be adherent due to previous caesarean sections, but there is always a lateral window available which has not been dissected previously and that could be the area that could be used in order to dissect the bladder safely away, which he demonstrated in this paper during vaginal hysterectomy. So, dissection now starts. This is an almost entirely unedited video and hence some of the landmarks during the dissection and the pitfalls will also be clearly visible.

The surgeon begins by separating the anterior and posterior leaf of the broad ligament, aiming for an avascular dissection to maintain a clear field of vision. The UV fold is incised below the scar tissue layer, and gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate loose areolar tissue from the lateral window below the UV fold, above the uterine artery. The uterine artery is identified as the first landmark, along with the bare surface of the anterior cervix.

The uterine artery is clearly visible, serving as the first landmark for dissection. The surgeon lifts the UV fold and makes small, gentle cuts close to the uterus to separate the UV fold. If the UV fold is loosely attached, it is dissected entirely before proceeding. The dissection focuses on the scar tissue junction with the uterus, avoiding the bladder, which is unlikely to be encountered at this height. The plane between the UV fold of peritoneum and the bladder is visible, allowing for incision of the UV fold to enhance bladder visibility. Additional stretch is applied to the uterus from the vaginal end to facilitate dissection of the UV fold, staying above the scar tissue to safely dissect the bladder.

The surgeon incises the UV fold of peritoneum from left to right, reaching the round ligament on the right. This dissection reveals the bladder fascia, allowing further dissection up to the midline. The right round ligament is already cut, and visible fat indicates the bladder's location. The surgeon develops the space on the right side, then returns to the left to dissect the bladder. Blood is suctioned to prevent staining of loose areolar tissue. Traction from the vaginal end keeps the bladder tense and separate from the cervix. The cervix appears white and firm, while the bladder is softer. The uterine artery is marked in red, scar tissue in blue, and the correct dissection plane with an orange arrow. The surgeon incises the scar tissue at its junction to the uterus, avoiding the bladder, which is below the cervix.

The surgeon identifies a slight color difference between the cervix and bladder, using this visual cue and the movement of the bladder and cervix to find the correct plane of dissection. The bladder is kept under stretch with a grasper while the surgeon gently lifts it up, continuing dissection close to the cervix up to the midline. The uterus is manipulated to facilitate the dissection, and once the midline is reached, the surgeon shifts focus to the other side, connecting the established plane of dissection. The technique involves lifting the bladder and performing blunt dissection in the loose areolar tissue to locate the uterine artery.

The surgeon gently incises the loose posterior peritoneum to locate the uterine artery and vein at the lateral border of the cervix. The uterine artery is identified below the grasper, while the bladder is visible above it. The surgeon carefully removes loose areolar tissue to expose the anterior surface of the cervix, ensuring the bladder is pushed away by cutting close to the cervix.

The surgeon uses traction provided by the assistant to dissect anterior to the uterine artery, revealing the white glistening surface of the cervix. The bladder scar tissue is incised close to the cervix, pushing the bladder downward. Small bites of tissue are taken with a harmonic scalpel or bipolar vessel sealer and cutter, ensuring the bladder is adequately stretched. The lateral space is identified, allowing for careful dissection of the anterior cervix surface and bold incision of the remaining scar tissue.

The surgeon gently dissects the bladder in small increments, gradually moving it away from the anterior surface of the cervix. The uterus is pushed cranially and kept in the midline to expose the tongue-shaped bladder tissue adherent to the cervix. Using a grasper in the left hand and a cutting instrument in the right, the surgeon confidently separates the bladder scar tissue, ensuring a clear field of dissection. The bladder is successfully dissected away from both sides, allowing the procedure to continue safely.
